text: The surgeon begins the procedure by addressing dense intra-abdominal adhesions using sharp dissection and electrocautery. The right crus of the diaphragm is carefully dissected away from the hernia sac and skeletonized down to the confluence, while ensuring the esophagus remains unharmed.
The surgeon performs sharp dissection at the angle of His, carefully separating the tissue in this region.
The surgeon uses sharp dissection to separate the gastric remnant from the gastric pouch, carefully avoiding injury to either structure during this meticulous process.
The surgeon repairs the parasophageal hernia posteriorly, carefully identifying and preserving both vagus nerves during the procedure.
The surgeon completes circumferential dissection around the esophagus, extending the dissection up to the level of the pulmonary vein to increase the esophageal length.

The surgeon notes that only two centimeters of the lower esophagus remain in an intra-abdominal location after dissection. A collis gastroplasty is performed, and the hernia sac is completely dissected and excised.
The surgeon approximates the right and left crura using non-absorbable sutures placed in an interrupted fashion, visually aligning the two structures.
The surgeon trims a biologic mesh to fit around the esophagus and secures it in place using a simple, non-absorbable suture.

The anesthesiologist inserts a fifty-eight French bougie to size the hiatus, which remains in place during the gastroplasty. The surgeon performs a partial gastrectomy using a sixty millimeter linear stapler.
The surgeon imbricates the staple line of the gastroplasty using a running barb suture, ensuring the staple line is reinforced and secured.
The surgeon performs a bubble leak test at the conclusion of the procedure, with no visible evidence of leakage from the repair site.
